event: The Baptism of Christ in the Jordan River
symbols: dove descending from heaven — the Holy Spirit manifesting at Christ's baptism in bodily form
water of the Jordan — ritual purification, death and rebirth, the passage into new life
John pouring water over Christ — the baptizer acknowledging the one mightier than himself
crowd witnessing — the public inauguration of Christ's ministry before the people
tree on the hilltop — the tree of life, the new Eden inaugurated by Christ's public ministry
description: A classical landscape painting by Nicolas Poussin depicting the Baptism of Christ. At center-right, John the Baptist in a red garment pours water over the head of Jesus, who stands bare-chested in the shallow river with hands clasped in prayer. A white dove descends from a break in the clouds above. Multiple figures surround the scene in various postures — some undressing to be baptized, others watching, some already in the water. The setting is an idealized classical landscape with a large tree on a hill in the background, mountains in the distance, and a luminous sky.
theological_description: Poussin's Baptism of Christ depicts the Trinitarian theophany described in all four Gospels (Matthew 3:13-17, Mark 1:9-11, Luke 3:21-22, John 1:29-34). As Jesus emerged from the water, heaven was opened, the Spirit of God descended like a dove, and a voice from heaven said, 'This is my Son, whom I love; with Him I am well pleased.' This moment is the public inauguration of Jesus's messianic ministry and the clearest revelation of the Trinity in the Gospels: the Father speaks from heaven, the Son stands in the water, the Spirit descends as a dove. John had been preparing the way as Isaiah prophesied (Isaiah 40:3), and now recognized Jesus as 'the Lamb of God who takes away the sin of the world' (John 1:29). Christ's submission to baptism, though He had no sin to repent of, fulfilled what He told the reluctant John: 'It is proper for us to fulfill all righteousness' (Matthew 3:15). Paul later interpreted baptism as participation in Christ's death and resurrection (Romans 6:3-4), making this Jordan scene the prototype of all Christian baptism. The diverse crowd witnessing the event reflects the universality of the Gospel invitation.

scripture_refs.reason: The baptism of Jesus by John, the heavens opening, the Spirit descending, and the Father's voice — the exact event depicted
John's declaration of Jesus as the Lamb of God — the theological identification occurring at this event
The voice crying in the wilderness to prepare the way of the Lord — John's baptizing ministry
Baptism as participation in Christ's death and resurrection — the sacramental meaning inaugurated here
Luke's account specifying the Holy Spirit descended in bodily form like a dove — the dove visible in the painting
